text: The surgical team places four 8.5mm ports in a straight line in the left lower quadrant for a robotic sigmoidectomy, utilizing the X-eye system for precise alignment.

The surgeon explores the donor cavity, removing adhesions and identifying the distal transection line near the recto-sigma junction. Using a vessel sealer, the descending colon is mobilized along the specified line towards the splenic flexure, with visual reference to the spleen and the tail of the pancreas.

The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to transect the distal margin, opting for a bipolar energy device instead of a stapler. After identifying the proximal division line with proper retraction, the mesentery is divided, followed by the division of the lumen of the descending colon.

The surgeon introduces a dilator of sizes twenty-nine and thirty-two, attempting to extract the bulky specimen trans-anally using ring forceps. Due to the specimen's size, the mesentery is split using a bipolar energy device, allowing for successful trans-anal extraction by dividing the specimen into pieces.
The surgeon separates the mesentery from the colon, facilitating the transient extraction of the colon. Another piece of the mesentery is removed, and the final specimen is extracted.
The surgeon places a 3-0 absorbable suture, each measuring nine inches in length, within the surgical field.

The surgeon performs a simple running suture starting in the middle, with each suture extending in opposite directions. The fourth arm is used for proper exposure and retraction in the pelvis. After completing the posterior line, the surgeon switches to a canal suture technique, sewing from outside in and inside out, with the fourth arm providing optimal exposure.

The surgeon uses indocyanine green (ICG) to evaluate the blood supply to the ends of the anastomosis, observing the fluorescent green glow indicating adequate perfusion. This technique is repeated twice during the procedure to ensure sufficient blood supply, allowing completion of the anastomosis without the use of staplers.

The surgeon completes the suturing by crossing each suture backward twice before dividing it. Air is insufflated through a sigmoidoscope to check for leaks, followed by suction irrigation. The anastomosis is visually inspected to ensure there is no tension or twist on the mesentery.
Operative time, minimal blood loss, fifty cc's, patient was discharged within forty-eight hours, and this is post-op picture. Thank you.
